rudiments:fiberpotential-bloom: Cut in bloom — rets from standard fibre, fine possible. No seeds.
rudiments:fiberpotential-mature: Fully mature — rets coarse to standard. Ripple for seeds and grain.
rudiments:fieldretting-empty: Empty. Lay rippled flax or cured nettle here to field-ret it.
rudiments:fieldretting-fine-eta: {0} bundle(s) retting — standard fibre. Fine fibre possible in ~{1}.
rudiments:fieldretting-stage-coarse: {0} bundle(s) retted — coarse fibre. Leave them longer for finer quality.
rudiments:fieldretting-stage-fine: {0} bundle(s) at FINE quality — collect them now, this won't last!
rudiments:fieldretting-stage-standard: {0} bundle(s) retted — standard fibre. Collect them before they rot.
rudiments:fieldretting-rotted: {0} bundle(s) left too long — rotted and ruined.
rudiments:stook-empty: Empty. Place fresh (unprocessed) or retted bundles here.
rudiments:stook-curing-progress: {0} bundle(s) curing: {1}% — arid air dries the green stems. Rain stalls this.
rudiments:stook-rain-warning: Warning: rain exposure is degrading the fibre quality!
rudiments:rettingvat-empty: Empty. Place rippled flax or fresh nettle in the vat to water-ret it.
rudiments:rettingvat-fine-eta: {0} bundle(s) retting — standard fibre. Fine fibre possible in ~{1}.
rudiments:rettingvat-stage-coarse: {0} bundle(s) retted — coarse fibre. Leave them longer for finer quality.
rudiments:rettingvat-stage-fine: {0} bundle(s) at FINE quality — collect them now, this won't last!
rudiments:rettingvat-stage-standard: {0} bundle(s) retted — standard fibre. Collect them before they rot.
rudiments:rettingvat-rotted: {0} bundle(s) left too long — rotted and ruined.
rudiments:rettingvat-lime-loaded: <font color="#c8b400">{0}× quicklime loaded — lime retting active on next batch.</font>
rudiments:rettingvat-lime-active: <font color="#c8b400">Lime retting — fast but quality capped at standard. Watch the rot timer!</font>
rudiments:blockdesc-rettingvat:
Legacy block — water retting now uses a sealed barrel; existing vats still work but can no longer be crafted.
Water-rets rippled flax or fresh nettle. Faster than field retting. Quality depends on timing — collect at the brief fine window for the best fibre, or leave it to rot.

Lime retting: right-click the vat with quicklime to load a lime modifier. One unit is consumed when a new retting batch starts. Lime makes retting 2.5× faster but quality is capped at Standard — fine fibre is never produced, and the rot window is tighter. Useful for bulk production when quality is not the priority.
rudiments:rettingbath-progress: {0} bundle(s) soaking in the retting bath: {1}%
rudiments:rettingbath-fine-eta: {0} bundle(s) retting in the bath — standard fibre. Fine fibre possible in ~{1}.
rudiments:rettingbath-stage-coarse: {0} bundle(s) retted — coarse fibre. Leave them longer for finer quality.
rudiments:rettingbath-stage-fine: {0} bundle(s) at FINE quality — collect them now, this won't last!
rudiments:rettingbath-stage-standard: {0} bundle(s) retted — standard fibre. Collect them before they rot.
rudiments:rettingbath-rotted: {0} bundle(s) left too long — rotted and ruined.
rudiments:scutcher-nopower: No mechanical power — connect an axle above or below
rudiments:itemdesc-flaxbundle-unprocesse: Right-click bare ground to place in a stook to cure the stems before rippling. Flax cut in bloom rets to finer fibre but carries no seed; fully mature flax rets coarser and yields seeds and grain when rippled.
rudiments:itemdesc-flaxbundle-cured: Stems are dried and ready for rippling. Ripple on a ripple block to comb out the seed heads (mature bundles yield seeds and grain; bloom-cut bundles yield none), then ret.
rudiments:itemdesc-flaxbundle-rippled: Seal it in a water barrel to ret it (fast) or lay it on the ground (field retting). Retting time decides the fibre quality.
rudiments:itemdesc-flaxbundle-retted: Dry it on a drying rack
rudiments:itemdesc-flaxbundle-broken: Scutch on a scutch board to remove woody shives
rudiments:itemdesc-nettlebundle-unproces: Stings bare hands. Right-click bare ground to stook-cure it before retting.
rudiments:itemdesc-nettlebundle-cured: Cured and ready to ret. Right-click bare ground to field-ret it, or seal it in a water barrel.
rudiments:itemdesc-nettlebundle-retted: Dry it on a drying rack
rudiments:itemdesc-nettlebundle-broken: Scutch on a scutch board to remove woody shives
rudiments:itemdesc-coarsefibers: Rough low-quality fibres from poorly-retted material. Suitable only for rope and basic cordage.
rudiments:itemdesc-finefibers: Smooth, strong fibres from well-retted and carefully processed material. Twist into fine cord for durability bonuses on bows and tools.
rudiments:itemdesc-finecord: A tightly-twisted cord of fine fibres. Use in place of flaxtwine in bow and tool recipes for a significant durability increase.
rudiments:itemdesc-nettlerhizome: A root cutting from stinging nettle. Right-click on fertile soil to establish a new wild patch — no farmland needed. Can be cooked on a fire.
rudiments:itemdesc-cookednettlerhizome: Roasted nettle root — earthy and filling.
rudiments:itemdesc-nettlefiber: Strong bast fibre - three make a length of twine
rudiments:itemdesc-nettleleaves: Young nettle leaves. Edible raw but the residual sting causes minor damage — cook them first to remove it completely.
rudiments:itemdesc-linseedoil: Pressed from flax seeds. Edible, and burns as lamp fuel
rudiments:itemdesc-linseedcake: Pressed seed residue - kindling or animal fodder
rudiments:blockdesc-nettlestub: The root crown left after cutting mature nettle. It will regrow on its own — or shovel it up to get a rhizome for transplanting.
rudiments:blockdesc-ripple-*: A tool used for extracting seeds from flax
rudiments:blockdesc-break-*: A tool used for breaking dried flax or nettle
rudiments:blockdesc-hatchel-*: A tool used for extracting fibres from broken flax or nettle
rudiments:blockdesc-dryingrack-*: Dries retted bundles. Warm, sheltered, dry weather works fastest; rain stalls it. Preserves fibre quality.
rudiments:blockdesc-fieldretting-*: Field-rets rippled flax or cured nettle laid flat on the ground. No crafting required — right-click bare ground with bundles. Damp weather advances retting; dry spells stall it (no quality loss). Watch the info text for the brief fine-fibre window.
rudiments:blockdesc-stook: Standing sheaves for curing or drying bundles outdoors. Cures green unprocessed bundles into cured stems (arid weather required). Also dries retted bundles, but rain will reset progress and degrade fibre quality — use a sheltered drying rack for a safe outcome.
rudiments:blockdesc-oilpress: Hold flax seeds against it to press out linseed oil and seed cake
rudiments:blockdesc-scutchboard-*: Scrapes woody shives from broken flax or nettle bundles, producing scutched bundles ready for the hatchel. Hold <hk>rightmouse</hk> with broken bundles to scutch them.
rudiments:blockdesc-mechscutcher: An axle-driven scutch mill. Feed it dried bundles and it automatically breaks and scutches them into scutched bundles. You still need to hatchel those bundles by hand to extract the final fibre.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-flaxbundle-u: Step 1 of 7: Right-click bare ground to place the fresh flax in a stook. Arid weather cures the green stems (about 1 in-game day). Rain stalls the process.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-flaxbundle-c: Step 2 of 7: Comb the cured flax by holding rightmouse on a ripple. Mature bundles give up their seeds and grain here; bloom-cut bundles have none to give.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-flaxbundle-r: Step 3 of 7: Ret the rippled flax by sealing it in a barrel of water (fast, controlled) — sealing immediately turns it into an open retting bath — or right-click bare ground to lay it out for field retting (slow, free). Bloom-cut bundles ret to standard fibre with a brief fine window worth catching; mature bundles ret coarse and settle at standard. Everything rots if left too long.
Step 4 of 7: Dry the retted flax. Safe option: a drying rack preserves quality. Risky option: right-click bare ground to stook it outdoors — rain will reset progress and degrade quality.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-flaxbundle-d: Step 5 of 7: Break the dried flax by holding rightmouse on a break.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-flaxbundle-b: Step 6 of 7: Scutch the broken flax by holding rightmouse on a scutch board to scrape off the woody shives.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-flaxbundle-s: Step 7 of 7: Extract flax fibres from the scutched flax by holding rightmouse on a hatchel. Higher fibre quality yields more fibre.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-nettlebundle: Step 1 of 6: Wear gloves — bare-handed handling stings! Right-click bare ground to place in a stook and cure the fresh stems (arid weather, ~1 day). Nettle skips rippling.
Step 2 of 6: Ret the cured nettle by sealing it in a barrel of water (fast) — sealing starts an open retting bath — or right-click bare ground to field-ret it (slow). Timing sets the fibre quality.
Step 3 of 6: Dry on a drying rack (safe, quality preserved) or stook outdoors (risky — rain degrades quality).
Step 4 of 6: Break the dried nettle on a break, then hatchel it for fibres.
Step 5 of 6: Scutch the broken nettle on a scutch board, then hatchel it for fibres.
Step 6 of 6: Hatchel the scutched nettle to extract nettle fibres.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-nettleleaves: Harvested from young nettle plants (stages 3–6). Raw leaves cause minor damage when eaten — the stinging compounds survive into the picked leaf. Cooking them in a pot removes this completely; nettle soup is a worthwhile early-game food. Handling: use the slow careful harvest (hold rightmouse) to pick without being stung, or wear gloves and break normally.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-coarsefibers:
Low-quality fibres produced when retting is too brief or conditions were poor. They lack the strength and smoothness of standard fibres, so they are only suitable for rough rope and cordage — not for fine cloth or bowstrings.

Use: coarse fibres can only be twisted into rope. They will not substitute for standard or fine fibres in twine, cloth, gambeson, or bowstring recipes.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-finefibers: The best-quality bast fibre, produced only by careful retting at the right stage. Twist four of them together into fine cord, which imparts a durability bonus when used in bow or tool crafting.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-finecord: A precisely-twisted cord of fine bast fibres — uniform, strong, and resistant to repeated stress. Replaces flaxtwine in bow recipes for a significant durability bonus (+50/100/150 by tier). Also used in linen gambeson head and leg armor crafting for +200 durability.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-nettlerhizom: A root crown dug from mature stinging nettle (stage 8 or 9). Hold a <hk>shovel</hk> and right-click the plant to carefully extract it — the plant is destroyed. Right-click fertile soil with the rhizome to establish a new patch anywhere without needing farmland. Wild nettle also spreads naturally by rhizome over time.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-nettlefiber: A strong plant bast fibre. Three nettle fibres spin into a length of twine - nettle is stronger than flax, so it needs fewer.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-linseedoil: Pressed from flax seeds on a seed oil press. A nourishing edible oil that also burns as lamp fuel.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-linseedcake: The pressed residue left after extracting linseed oil. Useful as kindling or animal fodder.
rudiments:craftinginfo-flax-text:
Two fibres, one craft
Rudiments turns flax and stinging nettle into usable fibre through a multi-step process.

Flax (7 steps)
Harvest timing matters — and flax drops nothing until it has bloomed. Cut it in bloom (stage 8) for the best fibre: those bundles ret from standard quality with a shot at fine, but carry no seed at all. Let it stand to full maturity (stage 9) and the stems coarsen like nettle — bundles ret from coarse and top out at standard — but the plant now drops seeds, and rippling the bundles yields more seed and grain for linseed oil.
1 - Stook-cure the fresh bundles: right-click bare ground to place a stook. Arid weather cures the green stems in about 1 day. Rain stalls this step.
2 - Ripple the cured bundles on a ripple to comb out the seed heads — mature bundles yield seeds and grain, bloom-cut bundles none.
3 - Ret the rippled flax. Field retting (free): right-click bare ground to lay them out flat. Water retting: fill a barrel with water, add the bundles and seal it — the barrel immediately reopens as a retting bath and the bundles ret faster than in the field. For a quicker lime ret (capped at standard quality, tighter rot window), make limewater first and seal the bundles in that instead. Dry spells stall field retting — no harm, just wait for rain.
4 - Dry the retted flax. Safe: a drying rack preserves quality. Risky: right-click bare ground to stook it outdoors — rain resets progress and degrades quality.
5 - Break the dried flax on a break.
6 - Scutch the broken flax on a scutch board to remove woody shives.
7 - Hatchel the scutched flax for fibres.

Nettle (6 steps)
Stinging nettle grows fast but needs careful handling. Young plants (stages 3–6) give edible leaves — hold rightmouse on the plant for a slow careful harvest with no sting; or wear gloves and break normally. Older plants (stages 7–9) yield nettle bundles.
Nettle skips rippling. Right-click bare ground with fresh bundles to stook-cure them first, then right-click again with cured bundles to field-ret, or seal them in a water barrel. Then dry, break, scutch, and hatchel for nettle fibres.

Fibre quality
Harvest timing sets the range, retting timing decides where you land in it. Bloom-cut flax rets straight to standard fibre and may open a brief fine window (the best fibre — more yield at the hatchel and durability bonuses on bows and armour) before settling back to standard. Mature flax and nettle ret to coarse first, improve to standard with time, and can never reach fine. Everything rots if abandoned. Water retting in a barrel is quick; field retting is slow. Watch the block's info text to catch the fine window.

Automation & oil
A mechanical scutch mill driven by an axle automatically breaks and scutches dried bundles into scutched bundles ready for hatcheling. A seed oil press turns mature flax seeds into linseed oil.
game:item-handbooktext-flaxfibers: Read the Plant Fibre Production Guide for a detailed explanation of turning flax and nettle into fibre.
rudiments:meal-nettle-note: Cooking removes the sting — nettle is nutritious and wholesome once heated.
rudiments:itemdesc-handcards: A pair of wire-toothed carding brushes for combing washed wool into rolags. Hold washed wool in your off hand and brush it between the cards.
rudiments:item-handbooktext-handcards: Hold washed wool fibers in your off hand and the cards in your active hand, then hold rightmouse to brush the wool into a rolag, ready for twisting into twine. Costs 1 durability per fiber carded.
rudiments:handcards-need-fibers: Hold washed wool fibers in your off hand to card them
rudiments:itemdesc-rolag-*: Washed wool carded into an airy roll, ready to be twisted into twine
rudiments:item-handbooktext-rolag: Made by carding: hold washed wool fibers in your off hand and brush them with hand cards (hold rightmouse). Four rolags twist into wool twine in the grid; a drop spindle or spinning wheel spins two per twine.
